subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Generated 2026-05-28 under v2.0: governance/politics rebuilt from independent/multilateral sources (IMF Art IV/SMP, World Bank MPO, UN/OCHA/UNHCR, V-Dem, Freedom House 2026, RSF 2026, HRW, ACLED, Crisis Group) + international press (Reuters/AFP/Al Jazeera/CNN); junta/state media EXCLUDED on governance, rights, and security claims.

Data limitation: a data-poor active-conflict state; security/territorial-control and casualty figures are external estimates with reduced confidence; the CFA franc is euro-pegged via BCEAO with a French convertibility guarantee.

Standout drags: a deteriorating jihadist war (JNIM blockade of Bamako and a 2026 FLA/JNIM northern offensive), a coup junta that dissolved all parties and self-extended indefinitely, and a Russia-Africa-Corps pivot with documented atrocities; the euro peg is the rare stabilizer. Net: clearly negative with a downside tail.

economic.notes: Gold-anchored and CFA-stabilized, but a mine-control dispute, a fuel blockade, aid cuts, and above-ceiling inflation weigh on it.

fiscal_state.notes: Growth ~5% on gold/agriculture but the deficit widened to ~3.4% on flood spending and aid cuts; an IMF staff-monitored program (2nd review Mar-2026) keeps a thin reform anchor. (IMF SMP 2026; World Bank MPO)

monetary_independence.notes: The CFA-franc euro peg gives stability and a French convertibility guarantee but zero autonomy, under the regional BCEAO. (IMF/BCEAO)

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: The CFA franc is not a reserve currency. (definitional)

inflation.notes: Atypically for the peg, Mali ran the WAEMU's highest inflation (~8% early 2025) on conflict crop losses and fuel-blockade disruption, above the 1-3% ceiling. (Ecofin/BCEAO; IMF)

banking_stability.notes: BCEAO-supervised and peg-anchored, but stressed by sovereign exposure and the conflict economy. (IMF)

trade_actions_capacity.notes: A top-tier gold and cotton exporter, but the state seized Barrick's Loulo-Gounkoto over a new mining code and the JNIM fuel blockade chokes import/supply lines. (Bloomberg; Reuters; Al Jazeera)

institutional.notes: A coup junta that dissolved all parties and self-extended, with eroding statistics and severe press repression.

rule_of_law.notes: Military rule by decree after the 2020-21 coups; all parties dissolved May-2025 and Goita granted a renewable 5-year term, with no independent judiciary. (Freedom House 2026; HRW; Al Jazeera)

statistical_integrity.notes: Still IMF-engaged via the staff-monitored program, but conflict and contested mining/aid data degrade reliability. (IMF SMP)

civil_service_capacity.notes: Weak and war-strained, with the state absent from much of the center and north. (World Bank; ICG)

press_freedom.notes: Among the steepest RSF declines; French outlets suspended, journalists detained, and critics intimidated. (RSF 2026; FIDH; HRW)

political_social.notes: Worsening insurgency, an indefinitely self-extended junta, and a large displacement/food crisis.

political_stability.notes: A JNIM blockade besieged Bamako (Sept-2025) and a 2026 FLA/JNIM offensive forced army and Africa Corps withdrawals from the Kidal region - the junta is entrenched but the security trajectory is sharply negative. (Al Jazeera; CNN; Soufan Center; ICG)

civil_liberties.notes: All political parties dissolved, protest and dissent criminalized, and enforced disappearances reported. (Freedom House 2026; HRW; Amnesty)

treatment_non_citizens.notes: Hosts ~138k refugees from Niger/Burkina amid its own displacement, but security and aid-funding gaps strain protection. (UNHCR 2025)

social_cohesion.notes: Deep north-south and Tuareg/Arab-vs-state cleavages, with Africa-Corps/army atrocities (e.g., Kidal) deepening grievance. (HRW; ACLED)

demographics.notes: Very young and high-fertility, but conflict, displacement, and weak schooling undercut the dividend. (UN; World Bank)

geopolitical.notes: Pivoted to Russia and out of ECOWAS into the AES, amid an intensifying war and Western disengagement.

alliance_reliability.notes: Exited ECOWAS (Jan-2025) for the Russia-aligned AES confederation, expelled UN/French forces, and depends on Africa Corps - isolated from the West. (Reuters; Amani Africa; observed)

conflict_involvement.notes: Active multi-front war with JNIM/ISGS plus the Tuareg-led FLA, including the 2024 Tinzaouaten defeat and a 2026 northern offensive. (Al Jazeera; ICG; ACLED)

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: No comprehensive Western sanctions, but mine seizures, an ICSID-style investment-dispute climate, and ECOWAS exit raise capital risk. (Reuters; observed)

physical_practical.notes: Severe Sahel climate stress, weak health, conflict-damaged infrastructure, and acute insecurity.

climate.notes: A highly climate-vulnerable Sahel state with recurrent drought and damaging 2024-25 floods, low adaptive capacity. (OCHA; FAO; IMF RSF-tier)

healthcare.notes: Fragile and aid-dependent, with ~400k SAM children regionally and services cut by funding gaps. (WHO/UNICEF; OCHA)

infrastructure.notes: Landlocked and import-dependent, with fuel/supply routes attacked and power/transport weak. (World Bank; Reuters)

crime_safety.notes: Among the world's least safe, with jihadist attacks, kidnapping, and banditry across much of the country. (ACLED; travel advisories)

disaster_insurance.notes: Negligible penetration against high conflict and climate exposure. (observed)

summary: Mali is a coup-belt junta whose security situation deteriorated sharply through 2025-26: a JNIM fuel blockade besieged Bamako and a Tuareg-led FLA/JNIM offensive forced the army and Russia's Africa Corps to abandon the Kidal region, while documented Africa-Corps/army atrocities deepen grievance. Politically the junta dissolved all parties in May 2025 and granted Goita a renewable five-year term, exited ECOWAS for the Russia-aligned AES, and severely curbed the press. The economy has a rare stabilizer in the euro-pegged CFA franc and large gold/cotton output, but the state's seizure of Barrick's Loulo-Gounkoto, aid cuts, and above-ceiling conflict-driven inflation cloud the outlook. With external data and contested security figures, confidence is reduced; the distribution is clearly negative with a real state-collapse tail.
